_about: Lexicon for 'A Blind Glass'. Tag a word in a passage with [[word]]; give it a different entry id with [[visible text|id]]. Ids match lower-cased. Type is 'concept' or 'verb'. Use … for elision. A passage with an empty title continues under the previous heading. An entry may carry an optional 'alt' list of alternate readings, each an object with its own gloss and source link (g, u, and optionally c, s, d); with Diffraction on, the glass can surface the primary reading or any alternate.

makes.g: Stein is thinking about poiēsis in the context of long debates about the difference between poetry and things made by hand: “Making is different from doing” (Nicomachean Ethics 1140a, trans. Rackham). For Aristotle, poiēsis, whose end is a product, is contrasted to praxis (πρᾶξις), whose end is the act itself. The cause of the made thing lies in the maker, not the made. Behind Stein’s refrain ‘all this makes —’, is the maker’s art.

alt.g: Vico in De Antiquissima Sapientia talks about “the maker’s knowledge” and argues that true learning is what is made with the hands: “verum ipsum factum — the truth is the made” (trans. Costelloe). According to Vico, we know something most fully when we have made it ourselves. God knows nature because God made it; humans know mathematics, institutions, laws, languages, and history because humans made them.

system.g: For Aristotle, knowledge is not a heap of facts but a system (systēma, a ‘standing-together’) with each science a demonstrative order rising from its own first principles, and inquiry as a whole divided into the theoretical, the practical, and the productive. To understand a thing is to see where it stands within that ordered whole: “Once you have surveyed our work, if it seems to you that our system has developed adequately in comparison with other treatments arising from the tradition to date — bearing in mind how things were at the beginning of our inquiry — it falls to you, our students, to be indulgent with respect to any omissions in our system, and to feel a great debt of gratitude for the discoveries it contains” (Sophistical Refutations 184b2–8, in Shields).

alt.g: Kant: “By the term Architectonic I mean the art of constructing a system. Without systematic unity, our knowledge cannot become science; it will be an aggregate, and not a system. Thus Architectonic is the doctrine of the scientific in cognition, and therefore necessarily forms part of our Methodology” (Critique of Pure Reason A832/B860, trans. Meiklejohn). See Kreines 2024.

resembling.g: David Hume posits that resemblance is a central type of philosophical relation: “It appears, therefore, that of these seven philosophical relations, there remain only four, which depending solely upon ideas, can be the objects of knowledge and certainty. These four are resemblance, contrariety, degrees in quality, and proportions in quantity or number” (Hume, Treatise 1.3.1.2, SBN 70). See also Wendy Steiner, who has explored resemblance in Stein’s work from the perspective of art.

alt.g: Descartes turns resemblance into a doubt. Of the ideas he takes “to come from things outside me,” he asks “why do I think they resemble these things?” — nature inclines us to assume it, but inclination is not proof, and even if an idea proceeds from an object it need not be like it (Meditations III). His example is the two ideas of the sun, the small one given by the senses and the vast one reasoned from astronomy, where the idea that least resembles the sun is the truer. Where Hume makes resemblance a foundation of knowledge, Descartes makes it the very place the mind is deceived.

difference.g: For Plato in the Sophist, sameness (tauton) and difference (heteron) are among the ‘greatest kinds’ that let one say a thing differs from another at all. Plato writes: “But what do we mean by these words, ‘the same’ and ‘other,’ which we have just used? Are they two new classes, different from the other three, but always of necessity mingled with them…” (Sophist 254e).

alt.g: Aristotle also treats sameness in the Topics: “Whether two things are ‘the same’ or ‘different’, in the most literal of the meanings ascribed to ‘sameness’ (and we said that ‘the same’ applies in the most literal sense to what is numerically one), may be examined in the light of their inflexions and coordinates and opposites. For if justice be the same as courage, then too the just man is the same as the brave man, and ‘justly’ is the same as ‘bravely’. Likewise, too, in the case of their opposites: for if two things be the same, their opposites also will be the same” (Topics VII.1, trans. Pickard-Cambridge).

interpretation.g: Aristotle’s treatise On Interpretation seeks to distinguish how language comes to bear truth and falsity, and offers an early typology of the difference between written and oral communication: “Spoken words are the symbols of mental experience and written words are the symbols of spoken words” (On Interpretation 16a3, trans. Edghill). This is very different from how modern understandings of interpretation work, probing the intrinsic meaning of texts or authorial intention. Stein seems to be parodying both frameworks for interpretation, as we cannot find logical statements that might be assessed as “truth,” nor her intentions.

believe.g: For Aristotle, knowledge is superior to belief (doxa): “he who has beliefs is, in comparison with the man who knows, not in a healthy state as far as the truth is concerned” (Metaphysics Γ.4, 1008b, trans. Tredennick). As Fred Miller notes, the Nicomachean Ethics (VI.5, 1140b26) characterizes practical wisdom (phronêsis, φρόνησις) as an intellectual virtue of the doxastikon (δοξαστικόν) — the capacity for belief.

analysis.g: Analysis (ἀνάλυσις) carries a textile metaphor: from ana- (up) + luein (to loosen), it means literally an unravelling or loosening-up. As Beaney traces it, to analyse is to unweave — to work backwards from a complex whole to its constitutive threads. Aristotle’s Analytica take their name from this procedure: the Prior Analytics analyses syllogisms into their figures and moods; the Posterior Analytics analyses demonstration back to its primitive, indemonstrable premises. The word names a reversal, a going-back. Stein’s "so rudimentary to be analysed" keeps the motion without the destination. See also the SEP entry on Analysis.

substance.g: For Aristotle, substance is what underlies and persists: “Substance, in the truest and primary and most definite sense of the word, is that which is neither predicable of a subject nor present in a subject; for instance, the individual man or horse” (Categories 2a11, trans. Edghill). Stein’s substances continually shift and destabilize.

sight.g: Seeing and theorizing are cognates in Greek; knowing is modelled on seeing. Plato: “the soul is like the eye: when resting upon that on which truth and being shine, the soul perceives and understands and is radiant with intelligence; but when turned towards the twilight of becoming and perishing, then she has opinion only, and goes blinking about” (Republic 508d, trans. Jowett). As Andrea Nightingale shows, Plato’s optics give the eyes a “pure fire … akin to the light of day” that streams out to meet the daylight “like unto like” (Timaeus 45b–d) with an image that William James seems to later retrieve with his “Stream of Thought.” Sight is also the root of philosophy itself: “no account of the universe would ever have been given if men had not seen the stars or sun or heaven … from these we have procured philosophy” (Timaeus 47a–b).

reason.s: Kant, Critique of Pure Reason, Preface to the First Edition
reason.g: Kant opens the Critique of Pure Reason by defining what Vernunft does: “Human reason, in one sphere of its cognition, is called upon to consider questions, which it cannot decline, as they are presented by its own nature, but which it cannot answer, as they transcend every faculty of the mind” (Preface, 1781, trans. Meiklejohn). Vernunft is the faculty that drives itself beyond experience in pursuit of the unconditioned — God, freedom, the soul — and so falls into antinomies and illusions of its own making. The “critique” is reason’s self-examination: a tribunal in which Vernunft must judge its own claims.

alt.g: Hume: “Reason is wholly inactive, and can never be the source of so active a principle as conscience, or a sense of morals” (Treatise 3.1.1.11). For Hume reason is wholly inert: it can tell us what is the case but never move us to act. Where Kant makes Vernunft the ground of knowledge, Hume strips reason of all motivating force — making Stein’s “sight of a reason” all the more vertiginous.

negative.g: For Spinoza, “finitum esse revera sit ex parte negatio” — to be finite is, in part, a negation — whereas to be infinite is “an absolute affirmation of … existence” (Ethics 1p8s1; see Hübner). For Hegel the negative becomes the engine of the dialectic (see Bowman).

alt.g: Where Spinoza makes negation the mark of the finite — determinatio negatio est — Hegel makes it the engine of all thought: “What propels the concept onward is the already mentioned negative which it possesses in itself; it is this that constitutes the truly dialectical factor. Dialectic, once considered a separate part of logic and, one may say, entirely misunderstood so far as its purpose and standpoint are concerned, thereby assumes a totally different position” (Science of Logic, trans. Miller, 4/21:39–40). The negative is not a privation or absence but an active force: reason is negative because it dissolves the fixed determinations of the understanding, and in that dissolution generates something new. Negation negated — Aufhebung — both cancels and preserves.

intention.g: Brentano revived the Scholastic notion of the “intentional inexistence” of an object: every mental act is “directed upon an object,” containing an object within itself. ‘Aboutness’ becomes the mark of the mental.

necessity.g: “‘Necessary’ means … that without which, as a concomitant condition, life is impossible … Again, what cannot be otherwise we say is necessarily so” (Metaphysics 1015a, trans. Tredennick).

alt.g: Hume dissolves Aristotle’s necessity by relocating it: “necessity is something, that exists in the mind, not in objects” (Treatise 1.3.14.22). We never perceive necessity in the world — only constant conjunction, one thing regularly following another. The feeling of necessity is an internal impression, a determination of the mind formed by habit. What cannot be otherwise turns out to be merely what we are accustomed to expect.

Kant accepts Hume’s challenge but refuses his conclusion: “the concept of cause implies a rule, according to which one state follows another necessarily” (Prolegomena, A91–92). Notwendigkeit is not in objects — Hume is right about that — but neither is it merely habit: it is a pure concept of the understanding, one of twelve categories through which the mind structures experience. Notwendigkeit–Zufälligkeit (Necessity–Contingency) sits in Kant’s table of modal categories as a condition of possible experience, not a discovery from it.

cause.g: “Now there are four senses which are most obvious under which all the causes just described may be classed: the material, formal, efficient, and final” (Metaphysics 1013b, trans. Tredennick). “Things are causes of each other; as e.g. labour of vigour, and vigour of labour — but not in the same way; the one as an end, and the other as source of motion. And the same thing is sometimes the cause of contrary results; because that which by its presence is the cause of so-and-so we sometimes accuse of being, by its absence, the cause of the contrary — as, e.g., we say that the absence of the pilot is the cause of a capsize, whereas his presence was the cause of safety” (Metaphysics 1013b).

fact.g: For William James, the hard particular against which an idea is tested. “The ‘facts’ themselves meanwhile are not true. They simply are. Truth is the function of the beliefs that start and terminate among them” (Pragmatism, Lecture VI). “No one can live an hour without both facts and principles, so it is a difference rather of emphasis; yet it breeds antipathies of the most pungent character between those who lay the emphasis differently” (Lecture I).

actuality.g: Aristotle: “The activity is the end, and the actuality is the activity; hence the term ‘actuality’ is derived from ‘activity,’ and tends to have the meaning of ‘complete reality’” (Metaphysics 1050a, trans. Tredennick).

wondering.g: Plato: ‘Wonder is the only beginning of philosophy’ (Theaetetus 155d3). See also Hunzinger and Llewellyn.

alt.g: Hunzinger writes: “Resulting from the conjunction of a perceived object and the perception that the subject trains upon it, thauma is neither an objective quality of the world nor a feeling separable, for the subject, from the perception of an ‘outside’ — what Greek depicts, like the English ‘wonder,’ through the use of an identical term for ‘something amazing’ and ‘feeling of amazement.’ Object of a subjective emotion marking ‘the emergence or occurrence of the other’ and the ‘mourning for the self as an independent entity’ (Irigaray 1984, 75), thauma is situated in this ‘in‑between,’ external to the being perceiving it but constructed by his subjective perception. Now, this perception entails the simultaneous awareness of two antithetical phenomena (what Neer 2010, 66, calls ‘perception of a synaptic joint’), of a gap between two poles, of a paradoxical duality. Thus, despite its referential diversity, the concept’s unity resides in the awareness of a paradox and the acknowledgment of a gap” (Hunzinger 423).

decision.g: “The cause of action — the efficient, not the final cause — is choice, and the cause of choice is desire and reasoning directed to some end” (NE 1139a, trans. Rackham). Irwin renders prohairesis as ‘decision’; behind it lies krinein (κρίνειν), ‘to cut, to judge,’ the root of ‘crisis’ and Kant’s Kritik.

alt.g: Behind Critique (Kritik) lies krinein — to cut, to judge, to decide. Kant's Critique of Pure Reason is not a system but an act of deciding reason's own limits: "a call to reason, again to undertake the most laborious of all tasks – that of self-examination – and to establish a tribunal, which may secure it in its well-grounded claims, while it pronounces against all baseless assumptions and pretensions" (Critique of Pure Reason, Preface). Where Aristotle's prohairesis is deliberative choice toward an end, Kant's critique is the wound of decision itself — reason forced to cut between what it can know and what it cannot, between the possible and the impossible. Stein's "wound in the decision" echoes this: not choice (prohairesis) but the incision, the judgment that divides.

means.g: Frege: “It is natural, now, to think of there being connected with a sign (name, combination of words, letter), besides that to which the sign refers, which may be called the reference of the sign, also what I should like to call the sense of the sign, wherein the mode of presentation is contained” (“On Sense and Reference”, trans. Black). The morning star and the evening star share a reference — Venus — but differ in sense: each presents the same object by a different route. “The reference of ‘evening star’ would be the same as that of ‘morning star’, but not the sense.” Stein’s ‘means’ rarely arrives at its reference: the sense keeps shifting, the mode of presentation multiplies, and Bedeutung recedes.

feeling.g: Kant: “Whether now the Judgement, which in the order of our cognitive faculties forms a mediating link between Understanding and Reason, has also principles a priori for itself … and whether they give a rule a priori to the feeling of pleasure and pain, as the mediating link between the cognitive faculty and the faculty of desire … these are the questions with which the present Critique of Judgement is concerned” (Preface, trans. Bernard).

recognition.g: Recognition is crucial for Hegel in the Phenomenology of Spirit, who distinguishes self-recognition from recognition by another. “Self-consciousness exists in itself and for itself, in that, and by the fact that it exists for another self-consciousness; that is to say, it is only by being acknowledged or ‘recognized’” (Phenomenology, trans. Baillie). For a longer history, see also the SEP entry on Recognition.

recurrence.g: For Nietzsche, the eternal recurrence is to will every moment as returning, identically, forever: “What if a demon crept after thee into thy loneliest loneliness … and said to thee: ‘This life, as thou livest it at present, and hast lived it, thou must live it once more, and also innumerable times’” (The Joyful Wisdom §341, trans. Common).

division.g: “That of dividing things again by classes, where the natural joints are, and not trying to break any part, after the manner of a bad carver” (Phaedrus 265e, trans. Fowler). To define is to carve a kind at its joints.

consider.g: To consider — Latin con-sidus — is to take the measure of a thing under the stars. “Look round at the courses of the stars, as if thou wert going along with them; and constantly consider the changes of the elements into one another; for such thoughts purge away the filth of the terrene life” (Meditations VII.47, trans. Long).

description.g: For Bertrand Russell, a phrase can mean without naming: ‘the present King of France’ denotes nothing, yet ‘the present King of France is bald’ is not nonsense — it is simply false. Since he is neither bald nor not-bald, “Hegelians, who love a synthesis, will probably conclude that he wears a wig” (Russell, “On Denoting”). Stein: ‘a description is not a birthday.’

reduction.g: Husserl names his method the phenomenological reduction: “the epistemological reduction must be performed, that is all transcendence that comes into play here must be excluded” (The Idea of Phenomenology, 1911). To reduce, in this sense, is to bracket — to put the natural world “out of action” and attend only to what is directly given in consciousness. A reduced description would be stripped of assumption, of worldly positing, of everything not immediately present to the mind. Stein refuses: “there is no occasion for a reduction … a reduced description is not a birthday.” The birthday — singular, unrepeatable — cannot be bracketed. What is given refuses to be reduced to what is merely given.

order.g: Plato: “He brought it into order out of disorder, deeming that the former state is in all ways better than the latter” (Timaeus 30a, trans. Bury).

alt.g: Aristotle: “Anaxagoras avails himself of Mind as an artificial device for producing order, and drags it in whenever he is at a loss to explain some necessary result; but otherwise he makes anything rather than Mind the cause of what happens” (Metaphysics 985a, 19).

succession.g: Hume sees the self as “nothing but a bundle or collection of different perceptions, which succeed each other with an inconceivable rapidity, and are in a perpetual flux and movement” (Treatise 1.4.6). Identity dissolved into succession.

quantity.g: “Quantity is either discrete or continuous” (Categories 4b20, trans. Edghill). How much, as against what kind; in Hegel, the leap from quantity into quality.

certainty.g: “This proposition, I am, I exist, is necessarily true whenever it is put forward by me or conceived in my mind” (Meditations II). The one thing the method of doubt cannot dissolve.

alt.g: Francis Bacon: “My method is hard to practice but easy to explain. I propose to establish degrees of certainty, to retain the evidence of the senses subject to certain constraints, but mostly to reject ways of thinking that track along after sensation. In place of that, I open up a new and certain path for the mind to follow, starting from sense-perception” (Novum Organum, p. 1, trans. Bennett). “In the interpretation of nature the mind should be thoroughly prepared and shaped up, so that it will at each stage settle for the degree of certainty that is appropriate there” (Novum Organum, Aphorism 19, p. 66). Bacon refuses both sceptical paralysis and premature system: knowledge advances by graduated induction, settling at each stage for the certainty the evidence warrants.

subject.g: The hypokeimenon, the ‘underlying thing’: “the substrate is that of which the rest are predicated, while it is not itself predicated of anything else” (Metaphysics 1028b, trans. Tredennick, who renders it ‘substrate’).

receptacle.g: Plato introduces a “third kind” alongside the eternal Forms and their sensible copies: “…that it should be the receptacle, and as it were the nurse, of all Becoming” (Timaeus 49a, trans. Bury). It receives without being any of the things it receives — formless, devoid of qualities, taking impressions as wax takes a seal. Later named chōra (space), it is described at 52a–b: “there is a third nature, which is space, and is eternal, and admits not of destruction and provides a home for all created things … we say of all existence that it must of necessity be in some place and occupy space.” The receptacle is not matter, not space in the modern sense, but the sheer capacity to receive — the ground that has no ground of its own. Stein places it beside symbol and monster, and then removes all three: “a receptacle and a symbol and no monster were present and no more.”

symbol.g: Augustine: “A sign is a thing which, over and above the impression it makes on the senses, causes something else to come into the mind as a consequence of itself” (On Christian Doctrine II.1) — smoke for fire, a footprint for the animal. The Greek sēmeion (σημείον) becomes Latin signum, and later Peirce’s symbol, the sign that signifies by law.

imitation.g: “This, then, will apply to the maker of tragedies also, if he is an imitator and is in his nature three removes from the king and the truth, as are all other imitators” (Republic 597e, trans. Shorey). Art as a copy of a copy.

alt.g: Where Plato makes imitation a deficiency — art three removes from truth — Aristotle makes it a primary human instinct: “Imitation is natural to man from childhood, one of his advantages over the lower animals being this, that he is the most imitative creature in the world, and learns at first by imitation. And it is also natural for all to delight in works of imitation” (Poetics I.4, trans. Bywater). The pleasure is cognitive: we learn by recognizing the imitation as an imitation. “An imitation, more imitation, imitation succeed imitations” — Stein’s repetitions of the word enact the Aristotelian point: each imitation teaches something the last one could not.

Kant makes genius the antithesis of imitation: “everyone agrees that genius is entirely opposed to the spirit of imitation” (Critique of the Power of Judgment §46, trans. Bernard). Genius produces original works that serve as models for others but “should not spring from imitation.” The genius gives art its rule as nature does — not by following rules but by exemplifying them. Stein’s “an imitation, more imitation” accumulates the word until it exhausts itself, performing the opposite of genius: imitation without an original, copying with nothing to copy.

alt.s: Hegel, Aesthetics: Lectures on Fine Art, Introduction
alt.g: Hegel dismisses imitation of nature as the aim of art: “mere imitation is superfluous” — the artist is never as good as nature herself, and such art draws all attention to the cleverness of execution rather than to spirit (Aesthetics, trans. Knox). True beauty is “the direct sensuous manifestation of freedom,” not a copy: art must give expression to Geist, not reproduce surfaces. Stein’s objects are not imitations of objects — a carafe, a box, a piece of coffee — they are the thing itself pressed into language, or its impossibility.

guess.s: C. S. Peirce, “A Neglected Argument for the Reality of God”
guess.g: Abduction: the mind’s knack for leaping to the right hypothesis among countless wrong ones. Peirce calls it, after Galileo, il lume naturale: “Modern science has been builded after the model of Galileo, who founded it on il lume naturale” (A Neglected Argument). Reason’s creative first guess.

act.s: Kant, Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
act.g: “Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law” (Groundwork, 1785). The moral law as pure form — and Stein keeps the form, empties the content.

truth.g: “To say of what is that it is not, or of what is not that it is, is false, while to say of what is that it is, and of what is not that it is not, is true” (Metaphysics 1011b, trans. Tredennick).

alt.g: Aquinas recasts truth as adaequatio — the conformity of intellect and thing. Where Aristotle puts truth in the statement, Aquinas holds that “truth resides primarily in the intellect, and secondarily in things according as they are related to the intellect as their principle” (Summa Theologiae I.16.1): a stone is called “true” as it answers to its idea in the divine mind. Truth is the fit — adaequatio rei et intellectus — between a mind and what it knows.

contradiction.g: “It is impossible for the same man to suppose at the same time that the same thing is and is not” (Metaphysics 1005b, trans. Tredennick) — the firmest principle of all. For Hegel, by contrast, contradiction becomes the engine of movement. Note Stein’s ‘both sides and the centre.’

alt.g: Stein speaks of contradiction as having “both sides and the centre” (TB 65). For Hegel the dialectical method, or “speculative mode of cognition” (Philosophy of Right §10), makes contradiction the engine of movement: “Hegel believed that reason necessarily generates contradictions, as we will see, he thought new premises will indeed produce further contradictions” (Maybee). The negative is not a failure of thought but its driving force.

harmony.g: “Men do not know how what is at variance agrees with itself. It is an attunement of opposite tensions, like that of the bow and the lyre” (Heraclitus, fr. 51, trans. Burnet). The fitting-together of opposites; for Leibniz, a harmony pre-established among substances.

motion.g: “The fulfilment of what exists potentially, in so far as it exists potentially, is motion” (Physics 201a, trans. Hardie & Gaye). Change as the actualizing of the potential; Zeno tried to argue it impossible.

change.g: Heraclitus made flux the law of all things. Plato reports him holding that “all things move and nothing remains still… you cannot step twice into the same stream” (Cratylus 402a, trans. Fowler); panta rhei (πάντα ῥεῖ), all flows. Stein’s table “means a whole steadiness” and yet asks “is it likely that a change” — the steady thing that still shakes.

alt.g: Parmenides denied that real change was possible at all; Aristotle’s reply made it intelligible: every change (metabolē) keeps a substratum that persists, so what seems to come from nothing in fact comes from what was already there potentially (Physics I.7).

Shields elaborates the hylomorphic analysis: every change needs a continuing subject and a form it gains or loses. A bronze statue is a compound of matter — the persisting bronze — and form, its shape; an accidental change such as a new coat of paint leaves it intact, but the loss of its essential form (melting the bronze down and recasting it) destroys it. Change, then, is the reshaping of a matter that persists (Shields, “Aristotle”).

repetition.g: “Repetition and recollection are the same movement, except in opposite directions” (trans. Hong) — what is recollected is repeated backward; genuine repetition is recollected forward. To take a thing again is not to recall it but to renew it.

suppose.g: The method of hypothesis: “I assume in each case some principle which I consider strongest, and whatever seems to me to agree with this … I regard as true, and whatever disagrees with it, as untrue” (Phaedo 100a, trans. Fowler). To suppose is to posit a ground ‘placed under’ the argument — Latin supponere, the exact twin of Greek hypotithenai (ὑποτιθέναι); and in medieval logic the doctrine of suppositio asked what a term stands for in a proposition (Ockham, Buridan). The master verb of the book.

measure.g: “Man is the measure of all things: of the things that are, that they are, and of the things that are not, that they are not” — Protagoras’ dictum, reported at Theaetetus 152a. Truth made relative to the measurer.

alt.g: Stein more likely means to conjure empirical science. “The history of measurement is the history of how societies made quantities comparable: bodies to fields, grain to silver, time to motion, voltage to constants, and sensor readings to shared data infrastructures … Measurement is not only a technical activity. It is a social agreement about units, instruments, calibration, authority, uncertainty, and permitted uses.” Across periods it enables practical coordination, administration, and science — because experiment depends on reproducible quantities and calculable uncertainty. See also Kuhn (1961).

shows.g: “By demonstration I mean a syllogism productive of scientific knowledge” (Posterior Analytics 71b, trans. Mure). Proof that exhibits why a thing must be so. Stein keeps the showing, withholds the proof.

religion.s: William James, The Varieties of Religious Experience
religion.g: William James: “The feelings, acts, and experiences of individual men in their solitude, so far as they apprehend themselves to stand in relation to whatever they may consider the divine” (Varieties, Lecture II). Religion taken first-personally, before any church.

singularity.g: Scotus’s haecceitas — literally ‘thisness’: the individuating principle, wholly without shared content, by which a thing is not merely human or equine but just this one and no other. The ground of the singular.

clearness.g: Descartes famously refers to ‘clear and distinct’ ideas in Meditations, in the passage where he discusses the wax: “This wax that is perceived by the mind alone is, of course, the same wax that I see, touch, and picture in my imagination – in short the same wax I thought it to be from the start. But although my perception of it seemed to be a case of vision and touch and imagination, it isn’t so and it never was. Rather, it is purely a perception by the mind alone – formerly an imperfect and confused one, but now clear and distinct because I am now concentrating carefully on what the wax consists in” (Meditations II).

alt.s: Peirce, “How to Make Our Ideas Clear”
alt.g: Descartes’ ‘clear and distinct’ idea is sharpened by Peirce into the pragmatic maxim: “A clear idea is defined as one which is so apprehended that it will be recognized wherever it is met with, and so that no other will be mistaken for it. If it fails of this clearness, it is said to be obscure. This is rather a neat bit of philosophical terminology; yet, since it is clearness that they were defining, I wish the logicians had made their definition a little more plain” (Peirce, “How to Make Our Ideas Clear”).

suffering.s: Schopenhauer, The World as Will and Idea, §57
suffering.g: Willing springs from lack, so to will is to suffer; and when desire is sated, “its life swings like a pendulum backwards and forwards between pain and ennui” (Schopenhauer, World §57, trans. Haldane & Kemp). All life is suffering.

legality.g: Kant distinguishes an action’s mere conformity with the law — irrespective of the incentive behind it — which he calls its legality, from its morality, where the idea of duty arising from the law is itself the incentive (Metaphysics of Morals, Introduction). Outward law-abidingness versus acting from duty.

name.g: Plato sets Cratylus’ naturalism against Hermogenes’ view that names hold only by agreement: “Everything has a right name of its own, which comes by nature … there is a kind of inherent correctness in names” (Cratylus 383a, trans. Fowler). Stein’s treatment of names is slippery; the titles hold the proper names of things but the poems underneath often feel disconnected.

education.g: For Plato, education “is not in reality what some people proclaim it to be” — not the inserting of sight into blind eyes, but the turning-around (periagoge, περιαγωγή) of “the entire soul … from the world of becoming … until the soul is able to endure the contemplation … of being” (Republic 518c, trans. Shorey).

giving.g: On how to give: not as an investment expecting return, but freely — “if a gift, it should be given in the way which will be of most service to the receiver” (On Benefits I, trans. Stewart). Here reduced to a bare oscillation.

justice.g: Plato: “To do one’s own business and not to be a busybody is justice” (Republic 433b, trans. Shorey). For Plato, justice means each part of soul and city are performing its own work, or alternately the right ordering of the whole.

likeness.g: Homoiōsis theōi (ὁμοίωσις θεῷ) means becoming like the divine. In Plato: “To escape is to become like God, so far as this is possible; and to become like God is to become righteous and holy and wise” (Theaetetus 176b, trans. Fowler).

alt.g: Where Plato makes likeness an ethical aspiration — the soul’s approximation to the divine — Aristotle makes it the ground of aesthetic pleasure: “Thus the reason why men enjoy seeing a likeness is, that in contemplating it they find themselves learning or inferring, and saying perhaps, ‘Ah, that is he’” (Poetics IV, trans. Butcher). To see a likeness is to recognize — to register both the similarity and the gap. The pleasure is cognitive: we learn what something is by seeing what it resembles. Stein’s “incredible likeness” in the closing of Rooms holds both senses at once: the soul’s aspiration and the reader’s sudden recognition.

room.g: In the 2nd part of Discourse on Method, Descartes situates himself in his room: “…having no cares or passions to trouble me, I remained all day alone in a heated room, where I had complete leisure to review my own ideas” (Descartes, Discourse, trans. Olscamp 11).

alt.g: Rooms are also an important image for Bacon: “Countless people have stamped around in nature’s outer courts; let us get across those and try to find a way into the inner rooms” (Novum Organum, p. 3, trans. Bennett). “At this time we are merely lingering in nature’s outer courts, and we aren’t preparing a way into its inner chambers. Yet no-one can give a body a new nature, or successfully and appropriately turn it into a new kind of body without first getting a competent knowledge of the body so to be altered or transformed” (Novum Organum, Book 2, pp. 1–25, p. 51). The interior of things — their microstructure, their hidden form — requires forcing a way past the outer courts of appearance.
